messages.content:
You are a question answering assistant. Your job is to generate a SPARQL query to answer a given user question.

You should follow a step-by-step approach to generate the SPARQL query:
1. Determine possible entities and properties implied by the user question.
2. Search for the entities and properties in the knowledge graphs. Where applicable, constrain the searches with already identified entities and properties.
3. Gradually build up the SPARQL query using the identified entities and properties. Start with simple queries and add more complexity as needed. Execute intermediate queries to get feedback and to verify your assumptions. You may need to refine or rethink your current plan based on the query results and go back to step 2 if needed, possibly multiple times.
4. Use the answer or cancel function to finalize your answer and stop the generation process.

Available knowledge graphs:
- "wikidata" at https://qlever.dev/api/wikidata
  - "entity" index (type="fuzzy"): Entities indexed by their labels and synonyms
  - "property" index (type="embedding", modalities="text"): Properties indexed by their labels, synonyms, and identifiers

Index types used:
- "embedding": Retrieves items by cosine similarity between their embeddings and the query embedding. The embedding model used depends on the index and may support text, images, or both.
- "fuzzy": Retrieves items by overlap between their label words and the query keywords. The query keywords must not match label words exactly, but some fuzziness is allowed. The longer a query keyword is, the more it can deviate from a label word and still be considered a match, though it will also contribute less to the overall score. No special query operators like AND/OR are supported.

SPARQL prefixes for use in function calls:
- bd: http://www.bigdata.com/rdf#
- cc: http://creativecommons.org/ns#
- data: https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityData/
- dc: http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/
- dct: http://purl.org/dc/terms/
- foaf: http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/
- geo: http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#
- geof: http://www.opengis.net/def/function/geosparql/
- gn: http://www.geonames.org/ontology#
- hint: http://www.bigdata.com/queryHints#
- imdb: https://www.imdb.com/
- math: http://www.w3.org/2005/xpath-functions/math#
- ontolex: http://www.w3.org/ns/lemon/ontolex#
- owl: http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#
- p: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/
- pq: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/
- pqn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/value-normalized/
- pqv: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/value/
- pr: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/reference/
- prn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/reference/value-normalized/
- prov: http://www.w3.org/ns/prov#
- prv: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/reference/value/
- ps: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/
- psn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/value-normalized/
- psv: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/value/
- qb: http://purl.org/linked-data/cube#
- qfn: http://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/function#
- ql: http://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/builtin-functions/
- qlss: https://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/spatialSearch/
- rdf: http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#
- rdfs: http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#
- ref: http://www.wikidata.org/reference/
- s: http://www.wikidata.org/entity/statement/
- schema: http://schema.org/
- skos: http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#
- v: http://www.wikidata.org/value/
- void: http://rdfs.org/ns/void#
- wd: http://www.wikidata.org/entity/
- wdno: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/novalue/
- wdt: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct/
- wdtn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct-normalized/
- wikibase: http://wikiba.se/ontology#
- xsd: http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#

Additional rules to follow:
- Explain your thought process before and after each step and function call.
- Do not ask the user for clarification until you have made an initial attempt at completing the task. When the task input is incomplete or ambiguous, proceed based on reasonable assumptions.
- Do not use "SERVICE wikibase:label { bd:serviceParam wikibase:language ..." in SPARQL queries. It is unsupported by the used QLever SPARQL endpoints. Use rdfs:label or similar properties to get labels instead.
- Always execute your final SPARQL query before giving an answer to make sure it returns the expected results.
- The SPARQL query should always return the actual identifiers / IRIs of the items in its result. It additionally may return labels or other human-readable information, but they are optional and should be put within optional clauses unless explicitly requested by the user.
- Do not stop early if there are still obvious improvements to be made to the SPARQL query. For example, keep refining your SPARQL query if its result contains irrelevant items or is missing items you expected.
- Do not perform additional computation (e.g. filtering, sorting, calculations) on the result of the SPARQL query to determine the answer. All computation should be done solely within SPARQL.
- For questions with a "True" or "False" answer the SPARQL query should be an ASK query.
who wrote old flames cant hold a candle to you

content.message:
I need to find out who wrote the song "Old Flames Can't Hold a Candle to You". 

Let me think through this step by step:

1. First, I need to identify the song "Old Flames Can't Hold a Candle to You" in the knowledge graph
2. Then find the songwriter(s) associated with this song

Since this is about a song, I'll start by searching for the song title in Wikidata to verify its existence and get its identifier.

I'll search for the song "Old Flames Can't Hold a Candle to You" as an entity in Wikidata.

tool_calls.name: search_entity

args.query: old flames can't hold a candle to you

tool_calls.result:
Top 10 entity alternatives:
1. Old Flames Can't Hold a Candle to You (wd:Q3350194):
  - is instance of musical work/composition
  - original song written and composed by Pebe Sebert and Hugh Moffatt, originally recorded by Joe Sun in 1978
2. Old Flames Can't Hold a Candle to You (wd:Q64917996):
  - is instance of music track with vocals
  - vocal track by Dolly Parton; 1979 studio recording; cover version
3. Old Flames Can't Hold a Candle to You / I Knew You When (wd:Q64918017, matched via "Old Flames Can't Hold a Candle to You"), also known as "I Knew You When", "Old Flames Can't Hold a Candle to You":
  - 1980 single by Dolly Parton
  - is instance of single
4. Old Flames Can't Hold a Candle to You / I Knew You When (wd:Q64918022, matched via "Old Flames Can't Hold a Candle to You"), also known as "I Knew You When", "Old Flames Can't Hold a Candle to You", "RCA – PB-12040":
  - 1980 US 7" single by Dolly Parton; RCA – PB-12040
  - is instance of 7″ single
5. Mind your language if you want to avoid a ‘right old stew’ (wd:Q129136079):
  - is instance of scholarly article
  - scholarly article
6. You hold a key to your patient's recovery (wd:Q69302840):
  - is instance of scholarly article
  - scientific article published on 01 July 1986
7. One "A" you don't want your kids to get (wd:Q78037088):
  - is instance of scholarly article
  - scientific article published on 01 May 2001
8. I Want to Draw a Cat For You (wd:Q23680086):
  - eccentric cartooning startup that gained recognition via an appearance on "Shark Tank"
  - is instance of website
9. You Can't Teach a New Phone Old Tricks (wd:Q127436186):
  - is instance of scholarly article
  - scholarly article
10. "Hold a candle to the sun" (wd:Q129659725):
  - is instance of scholarly article
  - scholarly article published on 30 April 1938

content.message:
I can see that there are multiple records for "Old Flames Can't Hold a Candle to You" in Wikidata. The most relevant one appears to be the song itself. 

Looking at the first result (wd:Q3350194), it states that the song was originally written and composed by Pebe Sebert and Hugh Moffatt. This seems to be the most direct answer to the question "who wrote old flames can't hold a candle to you?"

I should now verify this information by checking the specific song entity (wd:Q3350194) to confirm the songwriter details.

Let me get more information about this song entity.

tool_calls.name: search_with_constraints
tool_calls.result:
Top 10 property alternatives:
1. lyricist (wd:P676 as wdt/p, matched via "author"), also known as "author", "lyrics by", "songwriter", "text author", "text by", etc.:
  - author of song lyrics
2. performer (wd:P175 as wdt/p, matched via "artist"), also known as "actor", "actress", "artist", "dancer", "musical artist", etc.:
  - actor, musician, band or other performer associated with this role or musical work
3. composer (wd:P86 as wdt/p, matched via "written by"), also known as "composed by", "film score by", "musical score by", "music by", "original music composer", etc.:
  - person(s) who wrote the music [for lyricist, use "lyrics by" (P676)]
4. producer (wd:P162 as wdt/p), also known as "creative producer", "film producer", "musical producer", "music producer", "produced by", etc.:
  - person(s) who produced the film, musical work, theatrical ... [for production company, use P272, video games - use P178]
5. publication date (wd:P577 as wdt/p, matched via "publication"), also known as "air date", "airdate", "be published during", "be published in", "broadcast date", etc.:
  - date or point in time when a work or product was first published or released
6. genre (wd:P136 as wdt/p), also known as "artistic genre", "art style", "atlas genre", "film genre", "genre of literature", etc.:
  - creative work's genre or an artist's field of work (P101). Use main subject (P921) to relate creative works to their topic
7. language of work or name (wd:P407 as wdt/p, matched via "language"), also known as "audio language", "available in", "broadcasting language", "language", "language of name", etc.:
  - language associated with this creative work (such as books, ... (P103) and "languages spoken, written or signed" (P1412))
8. form of creative work (wd:P7937 as wdt/p, matched via "creative form"), also known as "album format", "album type", "art form", "artform", "artistic form", etc.:
  - structure of a creative work
9. title (wd:P1476 as wdt/p), also known as "article title", "full title", "headline", "known as", "original title", etc.:
  - published name of a work, such as a newspaper article, a literary work, piece of music, a website, or a performance work
10. instance of (wd:P31 as wdt/p, matched via "is a"), also known as "example of", "has type", "is a", "is an", "is of type", etc.:
  - type to which this subject corresponds/belongs. Different ... is an instance of mountain; volcano is a subclass of mountain

content.message:
I need to find the specific properties that contain the information about who wrote the song. From the search results, I see that "writer" could be represented by two properties:

1. P676 - lyricist (songwriter of lyrics)
2. P86 - composer (writer of music)

Since the question asks "who wrote" the song, it could be referring to either the lyrics or the music, or both. I need to check both properties for the song "Old Flames Can't Hold a Candle to You" (wd:Q3350194).

Let me search for the lyricist of this song.

tool_calls.name: search_with_constraints
tool_calls.result:
Call to function search_with_constraints returned an error:
Unknown index name 'object'

content.message:
I made an error in the function call. The index for object position should be "entity" since we're searching for objects (entities) of a property. Let me correct that and search for the lyricist of the song.
<tool_call>
{"name": "search_with_constraints", "arguments": "index": "entity", "position": "object", "query": "", "constraints": {"subject": "wd:Q3350194", "property": "wdt:P676"}}
</tool_call>

question: who wrote old flames cant hold a candle to you
